Illinois Senators Push FAA to Prioritize ORD for Staffing and Modernization

Illinois Senators Dick Durbin and Tammy Duckworth are urging the FAA to make Chicago O’Hare International Airport a top priority for staffing, technology upgrades, and infrastructure investment. In a letter to FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford, the senators cited ORD’s status as the nation’s busiest airport in 2025—with more than 857,000 annual operations—as proof of its critical role in the national aviation system. 

They’re calling on the FAA to address air traffic controller shortages, modernize systems, and fully utilize all available runways, including Runway 9R, to reduce delays and improve efficiency. The senators also encouraged the agency to give strong consideration to federal funding for ORD’s Terminal Area Plan, a modernization project that would expand gate capacity by about 25%. These investments, they say, are vital to maintaining safety, efficiency, and reliability for travelers across the country.

"The FAA Staffing Shortage Biden Left Behind Killed Two Pilots

Here's what the media won't tell you about what happened at LaGuardia Sunday night.

The FAA's air traffic controller workforce fell by nearly 2,000 employees between 2010 and 2024 – a 13 percent reduction. The Biden years were supposed to reverse that. They didn't. The FAA's own workforce plans show it was hitting constrained hiring targets that aviation safety experts said were far too low to meet actual system needs. The National Academy of Sciences concluded the FAA needed to maintain more than 14,000 controllers to handle demand. Biden's FAA was targeting 12,000 while actually running closer to 10,600.

The Senate's FAA reauthorization bill – the legislation that was supposed to fix controller staffing – spent nearly a year stalled under Democratic leadership. Senator Michael Bennet held the entire bill hostage to leverage a separate immigration deal. The FAA's authorization expired and had to be extended on stopgap after stopgap while pilots and controllers kept working in a system nobody was funding properly.

Biden also went 18 months without a confirmed FAA administrator while the staffing crisis was building. The agency drifted leaderless through near-miss after near-miss.

The result: LaGuardia, one of the most technically demanding airports in America – two intersecting runways packed into a tight Queens footprint with almost no margin for error – was four controllers short of its own target. And that was considered well-staffed compared to the rest of the system.

Former DOT Inspector General Mary Schiavo was direct about Sunday night: "Giving a fire truck clearance to cross the runway after an aircraft has been cleared to run in on final is a clear error. There's just no way around that."

Retired air traffic controller Harvey Sconick, who spent 38 years with the FAA, was more blunt: "The controller who crossed those vehicles while the airplane was landing just went brain-dead for a minute."

Controllers don't go brain-dead in a vacuum. They go brain-dead when they're managing two emergencies simultaneously on an understaffed overnight shift after years of forced overtime and back-to-back workdays – a direct consequence of the Biden FAA's failure to build the workforce the system needed.

Sean Duffy Shows Up at LaGuardia While Democrats Dodge the FAA Mess They Made

Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy was on site at LaGuardia Monday morning. He called the crash "horrific." He walked straight into the cameras and told the truth about what he inherited.

Duffy has been fighting to fix this since day one. He launched a plan to supercharge the controller workforce with financial incentives, expanded military hiring pipelines, and accelerated training resources. He called for additional funding Monday standing in front of the wreckage: "I've been asking Congress for additional money. We are modernizing our system, but we can't fully modernize it until Congress gives us additional money."

He's right. LaGuardia already has an advanced surface surveillance system – the alarm from that system is audible in the tower audio from Sunday night. The technology flagged the conflict in real time. A human being cleared the truck anyway – a human being stretched thin by years of understaffing that Biden and Senate Democrats allowed to fester.

The ALERT Act and the ROTOR Act – aviation safety bills responding directly to the 67 deaths over the Potomac – are still moving through Congress. Fourteen months of hearings, promises, and press releases while Duffy tries to rebuild a system Democrats ran into the ground.

Yes, FAA certification (Part 121 for major carriers) is a real regulatory barrier—expensive, rigorous, and time-consuming, requiring proof of safety systems, maintenance, training, and ops manuals. It's justified for passenger safety in a high-risk industry, not arbitrary favoritism.

That said, it's not abused to enforce monopolies or pick winners. Standards are uniform, publicly documented, and applied via transparent processes with appeals. New entrants like Breeze (2021) and Avelo (2018) have cleared it despite costs. True entry hurdles are often elsewhere: airport slots/gates leased long-term to incumbents, not FAA cert itself.

Deregulation since 1978 has kept the market competitive, with 12+ mainline carriers today.

FAA Restrictions and Construction to Disrupt SFO

SFO has commenced a six-month repaving project on Runway 1R/19L & 1L/19R and nearby taxiways, a critical infrastructure upgrade designed to improve safety and taxiway efficiency.

Additionally, the FAA has placed restrictions on its close parallel visual approaches to it’s east-west runways, known as the TIPP TOE and BRIDGE visual approaches to 28L and 28R, respectively. 

Yesterday the FAA released this statement, “San Francisco International Airport (SFO) will experience some flight delays due to a runway repaving project and an FAA safety measure. The airport’s runway project will put the two north-south runways out of service for approximately six months. The FAA safety measure prohibits flights from making side-by-side approaches to SFO’s parallel east-west runways in clear weather when the pilots acknowledge having the other aircraft in sight. It requires staggered approaches, with one aircraft offset from the aircraft on the parallel runway. The FAA never allowed side-by-side approaches in bad weather. The FAA is exploring ways to safely increase the airport arrival rate.”

🚦 Runway Status Lights (RWSL) — FAA’s silent safety guardian
#AC8646 #LaGuardia 

RWSL is an automated, surveillance-driven system—no controller input—using radar to detect conflicts and warn pilots in real time.

🔴 REL (Runway Entrance Lights)
RED → Do NOT enter (traffic on/approaching runway)

My recent article and blog highlight airport signage: shapes and colour. https://t.co/Gg8RJB5EGg
⚠️ Human Factors — Purkinje Effect
At night, the eye shifts sensitivity → green appears brighter than red
➡️ Green taxi/centreline lights may dominate visually
➡️ Critical RED RWSL cues can be under-perceived

Key point:
➡️ RWSL works independent of ATC clearance
➡️ It is a last-line safety barrier
➡️ RED always overrides clearance

At complex layouts (e.g. LaGuardia), multiple REL/THL layers protect intersecting traffic  

Bottom line:
Clearance ≠ Safety
If it’s RED → STOP.

The aviation analogy is worth taking seriously on the reimbursement side, not just the safety side. The FAA didn't just certify planes, it created the air traffic system that made commercial aviation economically viable. You can have a certified aircraft with no routes, no gates, no pricing structure.

That's the exact gap I found when I looked at the AI Medical Services Act. The bill builds a licensure frame, which is the certification layer, but leaves the revenue layer almost entirely blank. CPT code mechanics and relative value unit rate-setting are the actual gates to getting paid, and the statute doesn't touch them. A clinical AI tool can clear every board requirement and still have no viable path to reimbursement.

The ERISA problem makes this sharper. Roughly the majority of commercially insured Americans are in self-funded employer plans that state insurance mandates simply cannot reach. So the reimbursement framework the bill does sketch out is structurally blocked from applying to the largest insurance pool before it even starts.

The 40,000 to 124,000 physician shortage the AAMC projects by 2034 is the demand-side pressure everyone cites to justify moving fast. But if the revenue plumbing isn't built, certified tools sit idle in shortage areas anyway.

Aviation took decades to build the economic layer after the safety layer. Clinical AI may not get that kind of time, but what happens if we treat licensure as the finish line rather than the starting point for...

The YouTube ad opens with a montage of Fortnite and Madden gameplay, asking "Are you up for the challenge?" and promising an average $158,000 salary after three years.

The FAA currently has 11,000 controllers in service and 4,000 trainees in the pipeline, but remains roughly 3,500 short of the 14,600 needed.  Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy said the gaming focus "taps into a growing demographic of young adults who have many of the hard skills it takes to be a successful controller." 

This is the FAA's second attempt at gamer-focused recruitment after a similar 2021 campaign. The agency has gamified the application process itself, telling recruits to "level up your career" and comparing the application to a "quest."

"The FAA Staffing Shortage Biden Left Behind Killed Two Pilots

Here's what the media won't tell you about what happened at LaGuardia Sunday night.

The FAA's air traffic controller workforce fell by nearly 2,000 employees between 2010 and 2024 – a 13 percent reduction. The Biden years were supposed to reverse that. They didn't. The FAA's own workforce plans show it was hitting constrained hiring targets that aviation safety experts said were far too low to meet actual system needs. The National Academy of Sciences concluded the FAA needed to maintain more than 14,000 controllers to handle demand. Biden's FAA was targeting 12,000 while actually running closer to 10,600.

The Senate's FAA reauthorization bill – the legislation that was supposed to fix controller staffing – spent nearly a year stalled under Democratic leadership. Senator Michael Bennet held the entire bill hostage to leverage a separate immigration deal. The FAA's authorization expired and had to be extended on stopgap after stopgap while pilots and controllers kept working in a system nobody was funding properly.

Biden also went 18 months without a confirmed FAA administrator while the staffing crisis was building. The agency drifted leaderless through near-miss after near-miss.

The result: LaGuardia, one of the most technically demanding airports in America – two intersecting runways packed into a tight Queens footprint with almost no margin for error – was four controllers short of its own target. And that was considered well-staffed compared to the rest of the system.

Former DOT Inspector General Mary Schiavo was direct about Sunday night: "Giving a fire truck clearance to cross the runway after an aircraft has been cleared to run in on final is a clear error. There's just no way around that."

Retired air traffic controller Harvey Sconick, who spent 38 years with the FAA, was more blunt: "The controller who crossed those vehicles while the airplane was landing just went brain-dead for a minute."

Controllers don't go brain-dead in a vacuum. They go brain-dead when they're managing two emergencies simultaneously on an understaffed overnight shift after years of forced overtime and back-to-back workdays – a direct consequence of the Biden FAA's failure to build the workforce the system needed.

Sean Duffy Shows Up at LaGuardia While Democrats Dodge the FAA Mess They Made

Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy was on site at LaGuardia Monday morning. He called the crash "horrific." He walked straight into the cameras and told the truth about what he inherited.

Duffy has been fighting to fix this since day one. He launched a plan to supercharge the controller workforce with financial incentives, expanded military hiring pipelines, and accelerated training resources. He called for additional funding Monday standing in front of the wreckage: "I've been asking Congress for additional money. We are modernizing our system, but we can't fully modernize it until Congress gives us additional money."

He's right. LaGuardia already has an advanced surface surveillance system – the alarm from that system is audible in the tower audio from Sunday night. The technology flagged the conflict in real time. A human being cleared the truck anyway – a human being stretched thin by years of understaffing that Biden and Senate Democrats allowed to fester.

The ALERT Act and the ROTOR Act – aviation safety bills responding directly to the 67 deaths over the Potomac – are still moving through Congress. Fourteen months of hearings, promises, and press releases while Duffy tries to rebuild a system Democrats ran into the ground.

The conditions that killed those two pilots were not a surprise. The runway incursion history at LaGuardia was documented. The understaffing was documented. The pattern of overwhelmed controllers managing simultaneous emergencies on skeleton overnight crews was documented. The FAA's own safety review team called the staffing situation an "incident precursor" as far back as 2023.

Biden read those reports. His FAA leadership read those reports. Senate Democrats held the reauthorization bill hostage for nearly a year rather than funding the fixes.

Two young men who dedicated their careers to flying passengers safely are dead tonight.

Sean Duffy is doing his job. He was at the wreckage. He is demanding the funding. He is rebuilding a system his predecessor left broken.

The question is whether the same Democrats who spent four years underfunding the FAA will spend the next four years blocking the solutions – or whether two more dead pilots is finally enough to make them move."

FAA Targets Gamers to Close Air Traffic Controller Shortage

The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) and the U.S. Department of Transportation are turning to gamers in an effort to address the shortage of air traffic controllers in the United States.

Applications are set to open on April 17, with the goal of reducing staffing gaps across the sector. The FAA currently employs around 11,000 active controllers, with approximately 4,000 trainees in the pipeline. However, thousands more are needed for the system to reach full capacity.

U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy stated that new communication strategies are required to reach younger generations, noting that this gaming-focused approach targets individuals with skills such as rapid decision-making, focus, and the ability to manage complex processes.

The program offers paid training, federal employment benefits, and an average annual salary exceeding $155,000 after three years.

The FAA previously launched a similar campaign targeting gamers in 2021. According to Department of Transportation data, only about 25% of current controllers hold a traditional university degree, while roughly 65% of the U.S. population regularly plays video games. The agency believes that the cognitive skills developed through gaming can provide an advantage in high-intensity roles such as air traffic control.

A serious near miss at Nashville saw two Southwest Airlines Boeing 737s forced into emergency maneuvers after an air traffic control instruction put one aircraft directly in front of the other.

Flight WN507 was going around due to gusty winds when it was told to turn right, placing it into the path of departing flight WN1152. The aircraft came within roughly 500 feet vertically, half the required separation.

An FAA spokesperson confirmed the instruction “put the flight in the path of another airplane.”

Both crews reacted to onboard TCAS alerts, which overrode ATC commands and issued climb and descent instructions, allowing the jets to separate safely. Both flights later continued to their destinations without further incident.

The FAA has opened an investigation into the April 18 incident.

The air traffic control tower at Dublin Airport is Ireland’s tallest occupied structure, standing at 87.7 metres (288 feet) over 5 levels. Completed in 2020 and officially opened in June 2022, the tower cost €50 million as part of the airport’s expansion, including the addition of a second runway.

Designed by Scott Tallon Walker Architects and constructed by BAM Ireland, the tower has a distinctive conical shape and a glass façade that offers air traffic controllers uninterrupted 360-degree views of the airfield. To keep it stable two tuned mass dampers were installed to minimise wind-induced vibrations.

Incidentally if you're interested in becoming an air traffic controller, Ireland badly need more. 
Successful candidates undertake an 18 month training programme at AirNav Ireland’s state-owned facility in Dublin. It employs the majority of civilian air traffic controllers in the country. 
Meanwhile, the Irish Air Corps maintains its own controllers to manage military operations and airspace at Casement Aerodrome. 

As of 2026, AirNav Ireland employs 324 air traffic controllers across its centres in Dublin, Cork and Shannon. While Dublin Airport handles approximately 84% of Ireland’s flights, the Shannon ATC Centre manages over 90% of all North Atlantic air traffic that passes through Irish-controlled airspace without landing, covering a zone that extends as far as 15 degrees west of the Irish coast.

🚨 Still Time to Apply: Hundreds of Air Traffic Controller Trainee Positions Remain Open with the FAA!
The U.S. Department of Transportation and Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) are actively hiring Air Traffic Control Specialists (Trainees) — and applications are still being accepted.
Hundreds of openings remain as part of the FAA’s Fiscal Year 2026 goal to onboard 2,200 new controllers. The agency has already brought on nearly 1,200 this year, but spots are still available nationwide to help strengthen America’s aviation system.
This is a high-impact career with excellent pay and benefits — no prior air traffic control experience required. You’ll receive full paid training at the FAA Academy in Oklahoma City, followed by on-the-job training at facilities across the country.
Key Qualifications & Requirements:
•  U.S. Citizen
•  Under 31 years old on the announcement closing date (maximum entry age per federal law — exceptions may apply for prior qualified ATC or military experience)
•  Fluent English speaker (must be clearly understood over communications)
•  At least 3 years of general work experience OR a Bachelor’s degree OR a combination of education and experience totaling 3 years
•  Must pass the Air Traffic Skills Assessment (AT-SA) test, medical exam, psychological screening, background check, and drug test
•  Willing to relocate to any FAA facility based on agency needs (opportunities in every state, Puerto Rico, U.S. Virgin Islands, and Guam)
Training & Compensation:
•  Paid training from day one (starting ~$22.61/hour during Academy with housing support)
•  Competitive progression to six-figure salaries (average for certified controllers exceeds $155,000–$158,000 within a few years)
•  Full federal benefits, including early retirement eligibility
Application Deadline:
The window is open now through April 27, 2026 — or until 8,000 total applications are received (whichever comes first). Apply early — the window can close sooner if the cap is hit quickly!

🚨FAA Investigates Pilots Meowing And Barking Over The Radio At DCA 👇

The Federal Aviation Administration is actively investigating an audio recording from Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport, where pilots were heard meowing and barking over an air traffic control radio frequency.

In a viral audio clip, pilots operating flights in the area reportedly disrupted official communications by making continuous animal noises. The air traffic controller quickly became frustrated with the ongoing disruption, stating over the radio, "You guys, you need to be professional." The controller also criticized their behavior and career status by saying they were "still flying an RJ," an insult directed at regional jet pilots.

This type of disruption is a known problem in the aviation industry, specifically on the 121.5 MHz frequency, which is internationally designated for emergency use and monitored by all commercial aircraft. For years, an issue of unprofessional conduct has existed where pilots make animal noises on this frequency, despite strict rules against non-operational chatter. Aviation authorities consider it a severe safety risk because unauthorized transmissions physically block genuine distress calls from other aircraft. The FAA is now looking to identify the individuals involved, though enforcement remains difficult due to the anonymity of analog radio transmissions.

The US air traffic control shortage has become a national problem, but one former controller says the solution has been obvious for decades.

A FlightDrama reader who worked air traffic control in the United States Air Force from 1987 to 1991 says he left service fully trained and ready, only to find no clear pathway into the Federal Aviation Administration. He says the system did not know where to find qualified recruits then, and still does not.

The timing is striking. In 2025, the FAA is operating with roughly 3,000 fewer certified controllers than staffing models require, according to agency data, while delays linked to staffing constraints continue to rise across major hubs.

Former FAA administrator Randy Babbitt has said that military controllers “arrive with discipline, real-world experience, and far less training risk.” Yet there is still no direct fast-track from military ATC to FAA towers.

For some veterans, that gap meant walking away entirely. And for the system, it meant losing people who were already trained to keep the skies safe.

IS THERE A SHORTAGE OF AIR TRAFFIC CONTROLLERS?
Yes, not only is there a well-documented shortage, but the shortage was exacerbated by DEI policies.
* A 2023-2024 FAA workforce plan stated the agency was short 3,000 controllers to fill air traffic control stations and towers across the country. [a]
* Per a 2023 report issued by the FAA and Dept of Transportation:
- "FAA continues to face staffing challenges and lacks a plan to address them, which in turn poses a risk to the continuity of air traffic operations." [b]
- “We determined that 77 percent of critical facilities are staffed below the Agency's 85-percent threshold.” [b]
- “FAA cannot ensure it will successfully train enough controllers in the short term.” [b]
* In 2024 the FAA was sued in a class-action lawsuit by Former Nevada Attorney General Adam Laxalt, for rejecting over 1,000 qualified/trained air traffic controller applicants who’d all passed their tests, simply because of their race (they were too white). They were rejected because hiring them wouldn’t fulfill the FAA’s DEI quotas established in the Obama era. [c]
* Additionally, “the FAA then created a new test that included a biographical section, which was supposed to help add more African Americans into the air traffic applicant pool. The biographical questionnaire is supposed to have asked many irrelevant questions, such as the test-taker’s worst college subject and how many sports they played in high school, awarding extra points for certain attributes that were unrelated to their status as qualified air traffic controllers.” [c]
* “Up to that point, during the 20 years before the Obama-era FAA, the Collegiate Training Initiative (CTI) program graduated 100 percent of those who went on to work in their field. However, the aforementioned changes to the hiring process meant that many of these graduates began to be passed over in favor of applicants who had only graduated high school.” [c]
CONCLUSION:
There is indeed a shortage of necessary flight traffic coordinators, made worse via institutionalized racism against Caucasians, violating not only the people’s trust, their need to prioritize safety and competence, but also in violation of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which made it illegal to discriminate on the basis of race or gender amid the hiring process.

A serious near miss at Nashville saw two Southwest Airlines Boeing 737s forced into emergency maneuvers after an air traffic control instruction put one aircraft directly in front of the other.

Flight WN507 was going around due to gusty winds when it was told to turn right, placing it into the path of departing flight WN1152. The aircraft came within roughly 500 feet vertically, half the required separation.

An FAA spokesperson confirmed the instruction “put the flight in the path of another airplane.”

Both crews reacted to onboard TCAS alerts, which overrode ATC commands and issued climb and descent instructions, allowing the jets to separate safely. Both flights later continued to their destinations without further incident.

The FAA has opened an investigation into the April 18 incident.

$PLTR: THE U.S. IS UPGRADING ITS AIRSPACE BRAIN

So, the US government wants to replace an air traffic system that has been run by people for decades with a predictive, AI-driven decision layer.

The air traffic control is reactive these days. Controllers can only see a little bit ahead of them, so they have to deal with traffic jams and other problems. The SMART system changes that model by using AI to make predictions in real time. This way, the system can see hours ahead and fix the airspace before problems happen.

That's exactly where I see the chance for $PLTR. Palantir has always been strongest in settings like the following:

1) data that is broken up and very important
2) making decisions in real time
3) strict rules and safety limits

The failure is never accepted at the national level and systems must connect with dozens of old sources.

That's the area of $PLTR Foundry/AIP. There are only a few companies that can deliver at this level which makes this even more interesting. You have legacy players who have hardware and control systems and then you have these AI-native players who have the optimization models. But not many players can actually sit in the middle and orchestrate the entire system end-to-end.

If $PLTR gets a significant role in SMART, this will lock in civilian infrastructure, which is like defense but with more visibility and the ability to grow over time.

And then, these systems will become the backbone and will not get changed.

The air traffic control tower at Dublin Airport is Ireland’s tallest occupied structure, standing at 87.7 metres (288 feet) over 5 levels. Completed in 2020 and officially opened in June 2022, the tower cost €50 million as part of the airport’s expansion, including the addition of a second runway.

Designed by Scott Tallon Walker Architects and constructed by BAM Ireland, the tower has a distinctive conical shape and a glass façade that offers air traffic controllers uninterrupted 360-degree views of the airfield. To keep it stable two tuned mass dampers were installed to minimise wind-induced vibrations.

Incidentally if you're interested in becoming an air traffic controller, Ireland badly need more. 
Successful candidates undertake an 18 month training programme at AirNav Ireland’s state-owned facility in Dublin. It employs the majority of civilian air traffic controllers in the country. 
Meanwhile, the Irish Air Corps maintains its own controllers to manage military operations and airspace at Casement Aerodrome. 

As of 2026, AirNav Ireland employs 324 air traffic controllers across its centres in Dublin, Cork and Shannon. While Dublin Airport handles approximately 84% of Ireland’s flights, the Shannon ATC Centre manages over 90% of all North Atlantic air traffic that passes through Irish-controlled airspace without landing, covering a zone that extends as far as 15 degrees west of the Irish coast.

Nobody got hurt. But this is not the airport where you joke around on the radio.

🚨 Still Time to Apply: Hundreds of Air Traffic Controller Trainee Positions Remain Open with the FAA!
The U.S. Department of Transportation and Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) are actively hiring Air Traffic Control Specialists (Trainees) — and applications are still being accepted.
Hundreds of openings remain as part of the FAA’s Fiscal Year 2026 goal to onboard 2,200 new controllers. The agency has already brought on nearly 1,200 this year, but spots are still available nationwide to help strengthen America’s aviation system.
This is a high-impact career with excellent pay and benefits — no prior air traffic control experience required. You’ll receive full paid training at the FAA Academy in Oklahoma City, followed by on-the-job training at facilities across the country.
Key Qualifications & Requirements:
•  U.S. Citizen
•  Under 31 years old on the announcement closing date (maximum entry age per federal law — exceptions may apply for prior qualified ATC or military experience)
•  Fluent English speaker (must be clearly understood over communications)
•  At least 3 years of general work experience OR a Bachelor’s degree OR a combination of education and experience totaling 3 years
•  Must pass the Air Traffic Skills Assessment (AT-SA) test, medical exam, psychological screening, background check, and drug test
•  Willing to relocate to any FAA facility based on agency needs (opportunities in every state, Puerto Rico, U.S. Virgin Islands, and Guam)
Training & Compensation:
•  Paid training from day one (starting ~$22.61/hour during Academy with housing support)
•  Competitive progression to six-figure salaries (average for certified controllers exceeds $155,000–$158,000 within a few years)
•  Full federal benefits, including early retirement eligibility
Application Deadline:

The Federal Aviation Administration is actively investigating an audio recording from Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport, where pilots were heard meowing and barking over an air traffic control radio frequency.

In a viral audio clip, pilots operating flights in the area reportedly disrupted official communications by making continuous animal noises. The air traffic controller quickly became frustrated with the ongoing disruption, stating over the radio, "You guys, you need to be professional." The controller also criticized their behavior and career status by saying they were "still flying an RJ," an insult directed at regional jet pilots.

This type of disruption is a known problem in the aviation industry, specifically on the 121.5 MHz frequency, which is internationally designated for emergency use and monitored by all commercial aircraft. For years, an issue of unprofessional conduct has existed where pilots make animal noises on this frequency, despite strict rules against non-operational chatter. Aviation authorities consider it a severe safety risk because unauthorized transmissions physically block genuine distress calls from other aircraft. The FAA is now looking to identify the individuals involved, though enforcement remains difficult due to the anonymity of analog radio transmissions.

A serious near miss at Nashville saw two Southwest Airlines Boeing 737s forced into emergency maneuvers after an air traffic control instruction put one aircraft directly in front of the other.

Flight WN507 was going around due to gusty winds when it was told to turn right, placing it into the path of departing flight WN1152. The aircraft came within roughly 500 feet vertically, half the required separation.

An FAA spokesperson confirmed the instruction “put the flight in the path of another airplane.”

Both crews reacted to onboard TCAS alerts, which overrode ATC commands and issued climb and descent instructions, allowing the jets to separate safely. Both flights later continued to their destinations without further incident.

The FAA has opened an investigation into the April 18 incident.

$PLTR: THE U.S. IS UPGRADING ITS AIRSPACE BRAIN

So, the US government wants to replace an air traffic system that has been run by people for decades with a predictive, AI-driven decision layer.

The air traffic control is reactive these days. Controllers can only see a little bit ahead of them, so they have to deal with traffic jams and other problems. The SMART system changes that model by using AI to make predictions in real time. This way, the system can see hours ahead and fix the airspace before problems happen.

That's exactly where I see the chance for $PLTR. Palantir has always been strongest in settings like the following:

1) data that is broken up and very important
2) making decisions in real time
3) strict rules and safety limits

The failure is never accepted at the national level and systems must connect with dozens of old sources.

That's the area of $PLTR Foundry/AIP. There are only a few companies that can deliver at this level which makes this even more interesting. You have legacy players who have hardware and control systems and then you have these AI-native players who have the optimization models. But not many players can actually sit in the middle and orchestrate the entire system end-to-end.

If $PLTR gets a significant role in SMART, this will lock in civilian infrastructure, which is like defense but with more visibility and the ability to grow over time.

And then, these systems will become the backbone and will not get changed.

The air traffic control tower at Dublin Airport is Ireland’s tallest occupied structure, standing at 87.7 metres (288 feet) over 5 levels. Completed in 2020 and officially opened in June 2022, the tower cost €50 million as part of the airport’s expansion, including the addition of a second runway.

Designed by Scott Tallon Walker Architects and constructed by BAM Ireland, the tower has a distinctive conical shape and a glass façade that offers air traffic controllers uninterrupted 360-degree views of the airfield. To keep it stable two tuned mass dampers were installed to minimise wind-induced vibrations.

Incidentally if you're interested in becoming an air traffic controller, Ireland badly need more. 
Successful candidates undertake an 18 month training programme at AirNav Ireland’s state-owned facility in Dublin. It employs the majority of civilian air traffic controllers in the country. 
Meanwhile, the Irish Air Corps maintains its own controllers to manage military operations and airspace at Casement Aerodrome. 

As of 2026, AirNav Ireland employs 324 air traffic controllers across its centres in Dublin, Cork and Shannon. While Dublin Airport handles approximately 84% of Ireland’s flights, the Shannon ATC Centre manages over 90% of all North Atlantic air traffic that passes through Irish-controlled airspace without landing, covering a zone that extends as far as 15 degrees west of the Irish coast.

🚨FAA Investigates Pilots Meowing And Barking Over The Radio At DCA 👇

The Federal Aviation Administration is actively investigating an audio recording from Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport, where pilots were heard meowing and barking over an air traffic control radio frequency.

In a viral audio clip, pilots operating flights in the area reportedly disrupted official communications by making continuous animal noises. The air traffic controller quickly became frustrated with the ongoing disruption, stating over the radio, "You guys, you need to be professional." The controller also criticized their behavior and career status by saying they were "still flying an RJ," an insult directed at regional jet pilots.

This type of disruption is a known problem in the aviation industry, specifically on the 121.5 MHz frequency, which is internationally designated for emergency use and monitored by all commercial aircraft. For years, an issue of unprofessional conduct has existed where pilots make animal noises on this frequency, despite strict rules against non-operational chatter. Aviation authorities consider it a severe safety risk because unauthorized transmissions physically block genuine distress calls from other aircraft. The FAA is now looking to identify the individuals involved, though enforcement remains difficult due to the anonymity of analog radio transmissions.

🚨 Still Time to Apply: Hundreds of Air Traffic Controller Trainee Positions Remain Open with the FAA!
The U.S. Department of Transportation and Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) are actively hiring Air Traffic Control Specialists (Trainees) — and applications are still being accepted.
Hundreds of openings remain as part of the FAA’s Fiscal Year 2026 goal to onboard 2,200 new controllers. The agency has already brought on nearly 1,200 this year, but spots are still available nationwide to help strengthen America’s aviation system.
This is a high-impact career with excellent pay and benefits — no prior air traffic control experience required. You’ll receive full paid training at the FAA Academy in Oklahoma City, followed by on-the-job training at facilities across the country.
Key Qualifications & Requirements:
•  U.S. Citizen
•  Under 31 years old on the announcement closing date (maximum entry age per federal law — exceptions may apply for prior qualified ATC or military experience)
•  Fluent English speaker (must be clearly understood over communications)
•  At least 3 years of general work experience OR a Bachelor’s degree OR a combination of education and experience totaling 3 years
•  Must pass the Air Traffic Skills Assessment (AT-SA) test, medical exam, psychological screening, background check, and drug test
•  Willing to relocate to any FAA facility based on agency needs (opportunities in every state, Puerto Rico, U.S. Virgin Islands, and Guam)
Training & Compensation:
•  Paid training from day one (starting ~$22.61/hour during Academy with housing support)
•  Competitive progression to six-figure salaries (average for certified controllers exceeds $155,000–$158,000 within a few years)
•  Full federal benefits, including early retirement eligibility
Application Deadline:
The window is open now through April 27, 2026 — or until 8,000 total applications are received (whichever comes first). Apply early — the window can close sooner if the cap is hit quickly!

A major controversy has surfaced in Pune after allegations that several high-rise buildings were approved using fake Air Traffic Control (ATC) permissions.

Anant Gharat has claimed that fake or cancelled ATC letters were used to get permission for buildings in areas like Bibwewadi, Ambegaon and Sinhagad Road. He says these approvals may have been given with the involvement of builders, architects and officials from the PMC building department.

According to Gharat, letters received through RTI show that some permissions issued in 2020 were not valid and had already been cancelled by ATC authorities. He has warned that such illegal approvals could create a serious risk because Pune has sensitive locations nearby, including military offices, NDA and defence facilities.

Gharat submitted a written complaint to the PMC Commissioner on April 6, but says no action has been taken so far. He has demanded a high-level inquiry, criminal cases against those involved and immediate action against any illegal buildings. He has also asked Murlidhar Mohol to intervene.

There is, so far, little clarity about the occupants of the firetruck involved in the recent plane crash.

In the absence of clear information, explanations quickly emerge.

Some point to understaffed air traffic control and aging infrastructure.

From there, the argument expands: insufficient public investment, budget constraints, and ultimately taxation policy.

We are told that governments spend poorly, that taxes are too high—particularly for the wealthy—and that this constrains efficiency.

But this explanation only touches the surface.

A deeper structural question appears when we look at how wealth actually circulates.

Global billionaire wealth is estimated in the tens of trillions of dollars.

At the same time, large portions of this wealth are not held as simple income, but as financial assets and debt structures, which are often taxed differently—or not at all in practical terms.

Meanwhile, in the United States:
- Total GDP is on the order of ~$30 trillion annually
- A significant share of economic activity is tied to financial markets rather than direct production

This raises a key question:
How much of economic activity is generating real goods and services—and how much is circulating within financial systems themselves?

To understand the imbalance, imagine an economy as a flowing river.
If a growing portion of that flow is diverted into large, accumulating reservoirs—financial holdings that are rarely reintegrated into everyday economic life—then circulation slows where it matters most:
- wages
- infrastructure
- education
- public systems
- human development

The issue is not simply that wealth exists at the top.
It is that its circulation becomes structurally limited.

When capital concentrates without returning proportionally to the broader economy, it begins to shape more than markets:
- It shapes narratives.
- It defines what is considered “efficient,” what is “realistic,” and what is “unavoidable.”

Over time, structural issues—like underinvestment in critical systems—can be explained away through simplified stories, while their deeper causes remain largely unexamined.

At that point, the problem is no longer only economic.
It becomes epistemological—a question of how reality itself is understood.

If systems are analyzed only through measurable outputs, deficits, and costs, then the human dimension—well-being, coherence, long-term health—tends to disappear from view.

We become highly precise in measuring breakdown,
but less capable of recognizing what a healthy system would actually look like.
The result is a society that functions—sometimes impressively—
yet struggles to perceive its own imbalances.

Not because they are hidden,
but because the framework used to interpret them is incomplete.

🚨 UPDATE: Safety Regulator Mandates Fix For 586 Airbus Engines 👇

The US Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) has issued a critical safety order affecting major airlines flying the Airbus A320neo family. Released on January 12, 2026, "Airworthiness Directive 2025-26-04" requires urgent updates to Pratt & Whitney PW1100G engines to prevent a rare but serious fire risk.

While this legal order specifically targets 586 engines on US-registered aircraft, it is highly relevant to the global aviation community. The FAA is the primary certifying authority for these American-made engines, meaning other regulators like EASA (Europe) and DGCA (India) are most likely to follow suit with similar mandates for carriers worldwide.

The technical issue involves a domino effect. If a fan blade fractures during flight (often due to a bird strike), it can snap a critical support bracket. This structural failure puts stress on a high-pressure fuel tube, potentially causing it to crack, leak fuel, and start a fire under the engine cover.

In the US, this creates a logistical challenge for operators like Spirit Airlines, Frontier, JetBlue, Delta, United, and Hawaiian Airlines. Meanwhile, internationally, major operators of this engine type, such as Wizz Air, Lufthansa, and IndiGo, should be watching closely, as this maintenance will likely become a global standard.

Maintenance teams must remove a specific clamp within 30 days of 18th of Febuary, 2026, and eventually install redesigned brackets to eliminate the risk. This proactive move highlights why aviation remains the safest form of transport. Engineers and regulators are catching complex, "worst-case" scenarios and fixing them before they can cause an accident, a huge testament to how robust the safety mechanisms are in aviation today! ✈️

The aviation analogy is worth taking seriously on the reimbursement side, not just the safety side. The FAA didn't just certify planes, it created the air traffic system that made commercial aviation economically viable. You can have a certified aircraft with no routes, no gates, no pricing structure.

That's the exact gap I found when I looked at the AI Medical Services Act. The bill builds a licensure frame, which is the certification layer, but leaves the revenue layer almost entirely blank. CPT code mechanics and relative value unit rate-setting are the actual gates to getting paid, and the statute doesn't touch them. A clinical AI tool can clear every board requirement and still have no viable path to reimbursement.

The ERISA problem makes this sharper. Roughly the majority of commercially insured Americans are in self-funded employer plans that state insurance mandates simply cannot reach. So the reimbursement framework the bill does sketch out is structurally blocked from applying to the largest insurance pool before it even starts.

The 40,000 to 124,000 physician shortage the AAMC projects by 2034 is the demand-side pressure everyone cites to justify moving fast. But if the revenue plumbing isn't built, certified tools sit idle in shortage areas anyway.

Aviation took decades to build the economic layer after the safety layer. Clinical AI may not get that kind of time, but what happens if we treat licensure as the finish line rather than the starting point for...

Aircraft Charter Safety
Different types of flying:
Commercial (Part 121)
Charter (Part 135)
Private (Part 91)
General Aviation (Part 91)
 
The “Part ###” refers to the type of FAA Regulations each flight operates under
 
If you do a quick look up online or ask any AI currently available they will give you some statistics based on accident rate per 100,000 flight hours
Also going to throw in One vs Two pilot crews:
Single pilot crews have a 3X higher chance of an accident and about a 10X higher chance that the accident is fatal.
As of 2020:
The Part 121 commercial airline accident rate is about .23 accidents per 100,000 flight hours
The Part 135 charter accident rate is about .7 accidents per 100,000 flight hours
The Part 91 private world 4.8 accidents per 100,000 flight hours

Then General Aviation also Part 91 was about 7.0 accidents per 100,000 flight hours
 
Okay well that’s really not too unreasonable even at the extremely “high” end of General aviation which mostly encompasses farmer Joe out for a joy ride. Remember that people do dumb stuff all the time, and your limits and rules while operating under Part 91 allow you to do much more dangerous things. So, by assuming that higher risk, you will increase your chances of an accident and decrease your safety. That’s where the Part 121 and Part 135 regulations come into play. 
 
Part 121 vs Part 135 vs Part 91:
Part 121 airlines have much more standardization across the board when it comes to operators. Pretty much everyone follows the same rules, hires the same quality of people, and has strict operating parameters and routes. Combine that with intensive training and the safety features packed onto the aircraft and you have the safest form of transportation known to main kind. Now, that doesn’t eliminate people from doing dumb things and it certainly doesn’t reduce your risk to zero, but in 2026 we have much more technology available making travel that much more safe on airlines.

Part 135 charter operations are standardized but not to the same degree as airlines. Depending on your operator you’ll find wildly different hiring requirements and quality of pilots. This is why most people stick with the big expensive names like NetJets. NJ operates much more similar to an airline than most other Part 135 operations. Now, again, it comes down to the operator and the types of pilots they hire. Passenger jets, and turbo props are smaller and typically take more risk when flying. It’s all about serving the customer and getting them from point A to Point B. The types of pilots who work in the 135 world like the challenge and sometimes added risk of challenging airports. Due to the lack of standardized airports this makes for a much more dynamic environment compared to 121. More variance as I know you understand = more risk (not just in the markets). That’s why your accident rate almost goes to 3X in this industry. I will remind you though that it is still less than 1 accident per 100,000 flight hours. (Practically zero). Part 135 regulations require more experienced pilots, FAA oversight, and limit the types of weather and operations a company can do.

Part 91 private or “fractional ownership” is pretty much the wild west of aviation. The regulations are far less limiting meaning pilots can take even more risk than the Part 135 world. Maintenance standards aren’t really required, and there’s not “standardized” training required unlike Part 121 and 135. This is really the sweet spot for private flying, BUT you really need to vet the pilots and operators to ensure you don’t end up with someone who pushing the envelope beyond what you’re comfortable with. Just because something can be done legally, doesn’t mean it’s the smartest nor is it the safest. Most private jets are operated under Part 91, but depending on who runs the business drastically changes the safety. Most of the private jets you see crash are operated under part 91, and normally they crash due to someone cutting corners somewhere. Again legal, but not necessarily safe.
Who do you choose and why?
Most of your “charter” flights should be done under part 135 its up to you to find an operator you trust. Safety ratings and certifications do help, but they don’t show the whole picture. If you can get in touch with the owner of the operation that will tell you a ton about the type of business he/she runs. 
 
What aircraft?
Turbo prop (PC12, King Air, Piagio, TBM, Piper M600)

- You use these for trips that are about 1,000 miles. They stay low and while they can hit decently high speeds, that’s not the main benefit. They are robust and have great performance to price ratios. They won’t typically fly as high as jets, but that’s what makes them ideal for more regional trips
Light Jet (Cessna Citation M2, Phenom 300, Honda Jet)
- Typically you aren’t going to take more than 2-4 people on these and they only require 1 pilot. Now in my original reply you’ll remember 2 pilots is always safer than one. You can request an extra pilot if they don’t already require it but that will limit what/who you can take with you
Midsized Jets (Cessna Citation XLS, etc)
- You start to see two pilot crews required here (still capable of single pilot ops) but most places require two pilots. Also the experience required to fly one of these increases further making them safer than the light jets and turbo props in some cases. Again, it all comes down to the operator and types of pilots that they hire to crew the planes. These planes will also get you from point A to point B quicker in more comfort and increase your costs.
Super Midsized jets (Challenger 350)
- Two pilot crews required, typically 5-10 years of experience in the jet world to pilot one. Cost is much higher, but so are the capabilities and you can take 8+ of your friends. As far as a safety thing if you have the money, this is one of your best bets.

Heavy jets (Gulfstream G550, Challenger 650)
- Safety is basically negligible at this point compared to the super midsized. Pilots are extremely well trained and typically much more senior in their careers, but that doesn’t make them too much safer compared to the super mids.

Flying private comes down to a lot of things:
Budget
# of people

Speed
Comfort
Distance you need to travel
What you want onboard

I fly professionally and am dipping my toes into the turbine world more and more every year. I don’t worry about most things when flying because I know my training and I trust myself. That’s a similar mindset you’ll find in most pilots, but not all are as safety conscious. We are all mission and goal focused and I think that sometimes that gets the better of us and we make stupid decisions. That’s why having a hard limit set by the Part 121/135 world helps improve their safety records.
So if I was vetting a company for their safety mindset here’s what I would ask them: 
“What are your accident/incident reporting procedures?”
“Do you have an inhouse safety rep/expert?”
“Do you have a company ASRS program (aviation safety reporting system)?”
“How often do your pilots go to recurrent training?”
“What are the minimum hours and background experience to be hired here?”
“Who does your maintenance? And who supervises them?”
“What are your weather minimum op specs?”

🔴 WHY IT MATTERS:

Airline crews, Air Traffic Controllers, and support systems have been compromised—creating life-or-death safety risks.
DEI-driven hires and COVID-19 mandates have weakened aviation safety and put countless lives in jeopardy.
🚦 THE PLAN TO FIX IT:

1️⃣ Test all Air Traffic Controllers (hired after 2014)—those who fail will be terminated.

2️⃣ End DEI-driven policies—managers who enforced them will be removed.

3️⃣ Reassign executives: No more unqualified flight attendants managing flight operations. Managers will need proper industry experience.

4️⃣ Union overhaul: NATCA no longer controls FAA decisions.

5️⃣ Medical testing: Confidential testing and federal compensation for injured personnel after forced COVID shots.

6️⃣ Investigations into FAA medical staff—those pushing experimental medications and mandates will be removed.

7️⃣ Merit-based hiring only: Airlines and operators will make hiring decisions based on skill and experience—not HR-driven quotas.

8️⃣ Full pilot competency checks—until all pilots demonstrate the necessary qualifications.

9️⃣ AME doctors who pushed shots and mask mandates will lose their FAA approval.

🔟 Federal investigations: Into fraudulent hires, child trafficking, and corruption within FAA management.

✈️ The national airspace system depends on this reform to avoid a breakdown. We’ve seen what happens when safety standards are ignored—IT CAN’T CONTINUE.

The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) regulates airspace in the U.S. under the Federal Aviation Act of 1958 and the FAA Modernization and Reform Act of 2012. The FAA ensures the safety and efficient use of airspace, including air traffic control, aircraft certification, and airspace management.

The FAA also regulates drones under the Commerce Clause of the U.S. Constitution, which allows Congress to control interstate commerce. This includes regulating drones to maintain order and safety in congested airspace.

The FAA’s role is to maintain airspace safety and efficiency, including the regulation of drones, even at low altitudes where they could pose risks.

Yes, the Biden administration has recommended and enacted upgrades to the air traffic control system. 

President Biden signed a major aviation safety reform bill in 2024 that boosts air traffic controller staffing, increases funding for runway safety, and directs the FAA to deploy advanced airport surface technology, all as part of a $105 billion package over five years. 

Additionally, through the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law, the administration has allocated billions for airport and air traffic control infrastructure improvements, including grants specifically for modernizing air traffic control towers across the country.

The ROTOR Act (Rotorcraft Operations Transparency and Oversight Reform) is a bill introduced by Sen. Ted Cruz to boost aviation safety after the Jan. 2025 mid-air collision at DCA (Reagan National Airport), where American Eagle Flight 5342 hit a U.S. Army Black Hawk helicopter, killing 67 people.

In simple terms:
- Mandates military helicopters keep ADS-B transponders (tracking devices) on—no more turning them off, closing a loophole.
- Requires FAA reviews of helicopter routes, air traffic procedures, and coordination with military to prevent crashes.
- Aims to avoid future tragedies by improving oversight and transparency.

It's bipartisan and builds on prior safety efforts. For full text, check https://t.co/b0hTxH1oMm.

🚨FAA Investigates Pilots Meowing And Barking Over The Radio At DCA 👇

The Federal Aviation Administration is actively investigating an audio recording from Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport, where pilots were heard meowing and barking over an air traffic control radio frequency.

In a viral audio clip, pilots operating flights in the area reportedly disrupted official communications by making continuous animal noises. The air traffic controller quickly became frustrated with the ongoing disruption, stating over the radio, "You guys, you need to be professional." The controller also criticized their behavior and career status by saying they were "still flying an RJ," an insult directed at regional jet pilots.

This type of disruption is a known problem in the aviation industry, specifically on the 121.5 MHz frequency, which is internationally designated for emergency use and monitored by all commercial aircraft. For years, an issue of unprofessional conduct has existed where pilots make animal noises on this frequency, despite strict rules against non-operational chatter. Aviation authorities consider it a severe safety risk because unauthorized transmissions physically block genuine distress calls from other aircraft. The FAA is now looking to identify the individuals involved, though enforcement remains difficult due to the anonymity of analog radio transmissions.

🚨 Still Time to Apply: Hundreds of Air Traffic Controller Trainee Positions Remain Open with the FAA!
The U.S. Department of Transportation and Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) are actively hiring Air Traffic Control Specialists (Trainees) — and applications are still being accepted.
Hundreds of openings remain as part of the FAA’s Fiscal Year 2026 goal to onboard 2,200 new controllers. The agency has already brought on nearly 1,200 this year, but spots are still available nationwide to help strengthen America’s aviation system.
This is a high-impact career with excellent pay and benefits — no prior air traffic control experience required. You’ll receive full paid training at the FAA Academy in Oklahoma City, followed by on-the-job training at facilities across the country.
Key Qualifications & Requirements:
•  U.S. Citizen
•  Under 31 years old on the announcement closing date (maximum entry age per federal law — exceptions may apply for prior qualified ATC or military experience)
•  Fluent English speaker (must be clearly understood over communications)
•  At least 3 years of general work experience OR a Bachelor’s degree OR a combination of education and experience totaling 3 years
•  Must pass the Air Traffic Skills Assessment (AT-SA) test, medical exam, psychological screening, background check, and drug test
•  Willing to relocate to any FAA facility based on agency needs (opportunities in every state, Puerto Rico, U.S. Virgin Islands, and Guam)
Training & Compensation:
•  Paid training from day one (starting ~$22.61/hour during Academy with housing support)
•  Competitive progression to six-figure salaries (average for certified controllers exceeds $155,000–$158,000 within a few years)
•  Full federal benefits, including early retirement eligibility
Application Deadline:
The window is open now through April 27, 2026 — or until 8,000 total applications are received (whichever comes first). Apply early — the window can close sooner if the cap is hit quickly!

Palantir just won another major US government AI contract.

This time for air traffic control.

The FAA selected three firms to compete on SMART — the AI system that will manage US airspace: Palantir, Thales SA, and Air Space Intelligence.

Note the scope.

Palantir is now embedded in: DoD, CIA, ICE, IRS, HHS, US Army, US Navy, US Air Force, NYPD, and dozens of other federal and state agencies. Add FAA to the list.

Each contract individually looks narrow and technical. Airspace optimization here. Tax fraud detection there. Military logistics over here.

Stacked together, Palantir is building something different: a unified technical layer that touches nearly every interaction US citizens have with their government.

Taxes filed. Flights booked. Border crossings. Medical records. Military service. Law enforcement encounters. Social services applications.

Each of these generates data. Each is increasingly processed by Palantir infrastructure.

The SMART system specifically will scan every flight booking in US airspace to predict congestion. That's useful for reducing delays. It also means Palantir will have access to passenger manifests, booking patterns, travel frequencies, and movement data for every American who flies.

Paired with their other contracts, the data fusion potential is enormous.

This is the concern the original post is raising. Not whether any individual Palantir contract is justified. Whether the cumulative effect of Palantir being the common infrastructure layer across dozens of government functions creates surveillance capabilities that no individual contract disclosure would reveal.

US law prohibits agencies from sharing citizen data across departmental boundaries without specific authorization. Palantir doesn't need to share data. Palantir can just hold it across separate contracts, with separate access controls that nominally comply with each law individually while creating a technical possibility that the law was designed to prevent.

"Compliance with the letter while violating the spirit" is a known failure mode of complex regulatory architecture. Palantir's business model thrives in exactly that gap.

Alex Karp — Palantir's CEO — has been explicit about the company's strategic goal: become the operating system of the US government. Every major contract they win moves toward that goal.

Supporters argue this is efficient. One technical provider. Integrated systems. Faster government. Better outcomes.

Critics argue this is dangerous. One private company with unprecedented visibility into American civic life. Concentrated power without proportional accountability. A single point of failure for surveillance abuse.

Both sides have substantive points. Neither side is obviously correct.

What's not debatable: Palantir is winning. Contract by contract. Function by function. Quietly enough that most Americans don't realize how concentrated government data infrastructure has become.

The FAA contract is one more brick. The wall is getting tall enough to see from orbit.

If this concerns you, the political response requires naming the pattern specifically — not reacting to individual contracts in isolation. And so far, neither party has shown interest in doing that.

Democrats use Palantir extensively for immigration enforcement and other operations their base opposes in principle.

Republicans use Palantir extensively for surveillance and data analysis that traditional conservatives would have opposed in principle.

Palantir wins under both parties because both parties find the capabilities useful while neither wants to publicly champion the surveillance architecture being built.

That's how you end up with a private company quietly becoming the backbone of American government operations without either party having to defend the choice publicly.

The FAA SMART contract: fine on its own merits. Concerning only when you step back and see the forty other contracts that together produce something neither political party will name.

The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) regulates airspace in the U.S. under the Federal Aviation Act of 1958 and the FAA Modernization and Reform Act of 2012. The FAA ensures the safety and efficient use of airspace, including air traffic control, aircraft certification, and airspace management.

The FAA also regulates drones under the Commerce Clause of the U.S. Constitution, which allows Congress to control interstate commerce. This includes regulating drones to maintain order and safety in congested airspace.

The FAA’s role is to maintain airspace safety and efficiency, including the regulation of drones, even at low altitudes where they could pose risks.

•  U.S. Citizen

•  Willing to relocate to any FAA facility based on agency needs (opportunities in every state, Puerto Rico, U.S. Virgin Islands, and Guam)
Training & Compensation:
•  Paid training from day one (starting ~$22.61/hour during Academy with housing support)

🚨FAA Investigates Pilots Meowing And Barking Over The Radio At DCA 👇

The Federal Aviation Administration is actively investigating an audio recording from Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport, where pilots were heard meowing and barking over an air traffic control radio frequency.

In a viral audio clip, pilots operating flights in the area reportedly disrupted official communications by making continuous animal noises. The air traffic controller quickly became frustrated with the ongoing disruption, stating over the radio, "You guys, you need to be professional." The controller also criticized their behavior and career status by saying they were "still flying an RJ," an insult directed at regional jet pilots.

This type of disruption is a known problem in the aviation industry, specifically on the 121.5 MHz frequency, which is internationally designated for emergency use and monitored by all commercial aircraft. For years, an issue of unprofessional conduct has existed where pilots make animal noises on this frequency, despite strict rules against non-operational chatter. Aviation authorities consider it a severe safety risk because unauthorized transmissions physically block genuine distress calls from other aircraft. The FAA is now looking to identify the individuals involved, though enforcement remains difficult due to the anonymity of analog radio transmissions.

“This is part of efforts to modernize the outdated air traffic control system, aimed at improving safety and minimizing technology outages. The FAA has already secured $12.5 billion for this project but estimates a need for an additional $20 billion.”

Palantir just won another major US government AI contract.

This time for air traffic control.

The FAA selected three firms to compete on SMART — the AI system that will manage US airspace: Palantir, Thales SA, and Air Space Intelligence.

Note the scope.

Palantir is now embedded in: DoD, CIA, ICE, IRS, HHS, US Army, US Navy, US Air Force, NYPD, and dozens of other federal and state agencies. Add FAA to the list.

Each contract individually looks narrow and technical. Airspace optimization here. Tax fraud detection there. Military logistics over here.

Stacked together, Palantir is building something different: a unified technical layer that touches nearly every interaction US citizens have with their government.

Taxes filed. Flights booked. Border crossings. Medical records. Military service. Law enforcement encounters. Social services applications.

Each of these generates data. Each is increasingly processed by Palantir infrastructure.

The SMART system specifically will scan every flight booking in US airspace to predict congestion. That's useful for reducing delays. It also means Palantir will have access to passenger manifests, booking patterns, travel frequencies, and movement data for every American who flies.

Paired with their other contracts, the data fusion potential is enormous.

This is the concern the original post is raising. Not whether any individual Palantir contract is justified. Whether the cumulative effect of Palantir being the common infrastructure layer across dozens of government functions creates surveillance capabilities that no individual contract disclosure would reveal.

US law prohibits agencies from sharing citizen data across departmental boundaries without specific authorization. Palantir doesn't need to share data. You got it! Here’s what we know about Senate legislative efforts to improve Air Traffic Control (ATC) over the last 8 years:

One significant piece of legislation is the FAA Reauthorization Act of 2024, which was passed by the Senate in May 2024 with overwhelming bipartisan support (88-4) and was expected to pass the House and go to the President's desk.
Sponsors: This was a bipartisan effort led by Commerce Committee Chair Maria Cantwell (D-Wash.), Ranking Member Ted Cruz (R-Texas), Aviation Subcommittee Chair Tammy Duckworth (D-Ill.), and Ranking Member Jerry Moran (R-Kan.).
Intent: The bill aims to reauthorize the FAA and NTSB for five years. Its priorities include strengthening aviation safety standards, growing the air traffic controller and safety inspector workforce, boosting consumer protections, advancing technology and innovation, and modernizing the national airspace system. Specifically, it looks to increase the number of air traffic controllers, require the FAA to use new technology to prevent runway collisions, mandate 25-hour cockpit voice recorders on new planes, and make it easier for customers to get refunds for flight delays.
Fate: The bill passed the Senate on May 9, 2024. The Senate also passed a one-week extension to give the House time to pass the bill. The Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation scheduled a hearing for May 14, 2025, to review the progress and any delays in implementing this act one year later.

More recently, in the 119th Congress (2025-2026), there's the Air Traffic Control Workforce Development Act of 2025 (S.697).
Sponsor: Introduced by Senator John Hoeven (R-ND), along with Senator Jeanne Shaheen (D-NH) and Senator Jerry Moran (R-KS).
Intent: This bill seeks to address the shortage of air traffic controllers by improving training and retention. It proposes funding for tower simulator systems to reduce certification time, establishing incentive programs for trainees completing CPC qualification and for retaining current controllers, and improving mental health support for controllers. It aims to tackle issues like mandatory overtime caused by workforce shortages and the increase in near-misses at airports.
Fate: The bill was introduced on February 24, 2025, and was read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation. Its current status is "Introduced," and a summary is in progress. It has not yet left committee based on the information provided.

The video you mentioned from the earlier part of the thread shows a discussion about ATC system overhauls and upgrades, including a reference to House Republicans voting against upgrades during Elaine Chao's tenure as Transportation Secretary (2017-2021). While this highlights the political dynamics around ATC funding and modernization, it doesn't point to specific Senate bills from that exact period that left committee.

So, the FAA Reauthorization Act of 2024 is a major Senate initiative that did pass the Senate and addressed ATC improvements, while S.697 is a more recent bill focused on workforce development that is currently in committee.

Protocol Breach: Despite being involved in the fatal collision between a plane and a fire engine, the air traffic controller reportedly remained on duty, raising serious questions about FAA safety protocols.

FAA Bans Helicopter Flights Around Washington Airport

The U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) has imposed permanent restrictions on helicopter flights around Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport (DCA) following a crash over Washington’s airspace in January 2025 that killed 67 people. The decision was announced one year after a regional passenger jet operated by American Airlines collided in midair with a U.S. Army Black Hawk helicopter during approach.

After the accident, temporary helicopter restrictions had been put in place. With the FAA’s final rule, these measures have now become permanent. Under the new regulations, most helicopter and vertical takeoff and landing aircraft operations are prohibited in certain airspace near DCA.

The FAA stated that the accident occurred due to insufficient separation in a complex airspace where heavy commercial traffic intersects with military and civilian helicopter movements. Under the new rules, only essential missions—such as medical evacuation, law enforcement, presidential operations, and national security flights—are exempt from the ban.

Reagan (DCA) crash investigation update. 

The NTSB makes urgent recommendations to the FAA:

1 - Prohibit helicopter operations on Rt. 4 between Hains Point and the Wilson Bridge when Runways 15/33 are in use.
2 - Designate an alternative helicopter route when that segment is closed.

In its 10-page urgent recommendation report, the NTSB said that helicopters transiting the Route 4 helicopter corridor at the max authorized altitude of 200 feet could have only about 75 feet or less of vertical separation from an airplane on landing approach to Runway 33. The NTSB concludes the existing separation is insufficient and poses an intolerable risk to aviation safety.  

"Runway 15/33 only accounts for 5% of departures and 4% of arrivals, respectively, so the prohibition of using Route 4 would likely be infrequent."

FAA Plans to Hire Nearly 9,000 Air Traffic Controllers in Three Years

Following the 43 day government shutdown in the United States, the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) announced that it will accelerate air traffic controller hiring to address long standing staffing shortages. The agency has approved a new training pathway called the Enhanced AT-CTI program and aims to recruit around 9,000 new controllers by 2028. The program is currently offered at nine universities, allowing students to apply directly without attending the FAA Academy by completing an equivalent curriculum.

The longest government shutdown in U.S. history made the chronic staffing gaps in control towers more visible. Many controllers were required to work without pay, and as the shutdown dragged on, some were unable to report for duty. This led to flight delays, cancellations, and even a temporary reduction in commercial flight capacity by the FAA. Although operations have since normalized, the incident exposed how vulnerable the system can be.

The country is currently estimated to have a shortage of around 3,000 air traffic controllers. At many major airports, six day workweeks and mandatory overtime have become standard, with even a single absence capable of disrupting operations.
